figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustrative picture of a cone around a one-dimensional subspace V in R3.

Figure 2: Performance illustration: For the trials in (a) and (b) reservoirs of size N = 100 are used. For the DAR designed B, MSE in the first 100 steps: 0.029979 (using neural network readout) and 0.069852 (ridge readout). In (c), shaded bands indicate the 10th–90th percentile range across trials (central 80% variability), while solid lines show the mean error.

Figure 3: Normalized histogram of ridge regression mode contributions ⟨qi, c⟩2/(λi + λ), where {λi, qi} are the eigenpairs of the empirical state covariance matrix Σ and c is the state–target cross-covariance vector. The blue bars correspond to the data-aligned (designed) reservoir matrix B, while the black bars correspond to a random orthogonal B.

Figure 4: Performance illustration: For the trials in (a) and (b) reservoirs of size N = 100 are used. For the DAR designed B, MSE in the first 100 steps for the ridge regression readout: 0.005720 (for Random B) and 0.000110 (ridge regression training). In (c), shaded bands indicate the 10th–90th percentile range across trials (central 80% variability), while solid lines show the mean error.

Figure 5: Mackey-Glass System: Normalized histogram of ridge regression mode contributions ⟨qi, c⟩2/(λi + λ), where {λi, qi} are the eigenpairs of the empirical state covariance matrix Σ and c is the state–target cross-covariance vector. The blue bars correspond to the data-aligned (designed) reservoir matrix B, while the black bars correspond to a random orthogonal B.

Figure 6: Full logistic map: Normalized histogram of ridge regression mode contributions ⟨qi, c⟩2/(λi+λ), where {λi, qi} are the eigenpairs of the empirical state covariance matrix Σ and c is the state–target cross-covariance vector. The blue bars correspond to the data-aligned (designed) reservoir matrix B, while the black bars correspond to a random orthogonal B.
